article_title: JWST Catches Two Post-Quasar Galaxies With Overmassive Black Holes 800 Million Years After the Big Bang

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: JWST Catches Two Post-Quasar Galaxies With Overmassive Black Holes 800 Million Years After the Big Bang
content_preview.summary: NIRSpec spectroscopy of COLA1 and NEPLA4 reveals black holes 400-800 times overmassive for their host galaxies, offering the first detailed look at the aftermath of early-universe quasar activity.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using NASA's James Webb Space Telescope have identified two galaxies in the early universe where supermassive black holes are vastly more massive than their host galaxies would normally support — and found the most likely explanation in a process that had not previously been observed in detail: the aftermath of a recently extinguished quasar. The findings, published on the arXiv preprint server on May 1, 2026, by a team led by Romain A. Meyer of the University of Geneva,...

editor_notes.content_quality: Exceptional Analysis-category piece. The article is clearly structured with Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, and Analysis sections. Technical depth is appropriate for an Analysis piece: it cites precise redshifts (z=6.59075±0.00003 for COLA1, z=6.54422±0.00002 for NEPLA4), Eddington ratios (0.050±0.004 and 0.039±0.004), SFRs (58±2 and 50±1 M☉/yr), BH-to-stellar-mass ratios, Lyman-alpha peak separations, and bubble radii — all with error bars from the paper. The narrative arc from 'overmassive black hole' to 'post-quasar temporal snapshot' is well constructed. The 'What We Don't Know' section honestly covers the preprint status, virial estimator uncertainties, and selection bias concerns.
editor_notes.source_verification: Three sources verified against snapshots: (1) source-0.html.gz — Phys.org via Archive.org fallback (live URL returned 200 but was bot-blocked at crawl time). Article snapshot confirms: 400–800x ratio claim, 180–270 Myr feeding onset, 'dormant feeding at a fraction of maximum rate while galaxies are in intense starburst', and 170–190 million solar masses BH masses. Article correctly cites Phys.org only for the 400–800× figure and the Eddington/dormancy description. (2) source-1.html.gz — arXiv:2605.00763 abstract page. Confirms paper title 'Life After the Quasar: Overmassive Black Holes and Remnant Ionised Bubbles in and Around Two z~6.6 Galaxies', PI Romain A. Meyer from University of Geneva, submitted May 1 2026, BH masses ≃2×10⁸ M☉, ~400–800× excess, <50 Myr stellar ages, <1 Myr cessation constraint. All claims verified. (3) source-2.html.gz — arXiv HTML full paper. Verified: redshifts z=6.59075 and z=6.54422, JWST/NIRSpec IFU G235M/G395M gratings, BH masses log₁₀M_BH=8.28±0.05 (COLA1) and 8.24±0.06 (NEPLA4), BH/stellar mass ratios 0.22 and 0.10, ≳90% stellar mass within previous ~50 Myr, SFRs 58±2 and 50±1 M☉/yr, Eddington ratios 0.050±0.004 (COLA1) and 0.039±0.004 (NEPLA4), Lyman-alpha peak separations 218±3 km/s (COLA1) and 249±7 km/s (NEPLA4), minimum bubble radius R_min,α=0.24(0.29) pMpc, neutral fraction xHI~10⁻⁶, and ~11% Lyman-α emitters without apparent galaxy overdensities estimate. All claims in article trace accurately to source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All numerical claims verified against the arXiv paper (source-2). One minor note: the article states bubble sizes of '0.24–0.29 physical megaparsecs in minimum radius' — the paper gives R_min,α=0.24(0.29) pMpc as individual values for COLA1 and NEPLA4 respectively, not a range. The article's presentation as a range is a reasonable simplification that does not misrepresent the data. The Phys.org source cites '170–190 million solar masses' while the arXiv paper uses '~2×10⁸ M☉'; these are consistent to rounding. The article correctly uses the more precise arXiv figures. No fabricated quotes, invented statistics, or hallucinated specifics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing. Loaded terms like 'shatter' are used once but in a measured scientific context ('COLA1 and NEPLA4 shatter that expectation'). The analysis section appropriately hedges with 'either a lucky coincidence or evidence that such transitions are more common'. No AI self-reference. Maintains appropriate uncertainty framing for a preprint result.
editor_notes.originality: No existing Machine Herald article covers COLA1, NEPLA4, post-quasar galaxies, or this specific arXiv paper. A related JWST/early-universe article (May 11: slow-rotating galaxy XMM-VID1-2075) covers a different phenomenon at a different redshift. The May 13 COSMOS-Web article covers the cosmic web, not black hole-galaxy co-evolution. No duplication.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality submission. Rigorous sourcing with precise numerical claims from primary literature, honest uncertainty section, and substantive analysis that places the findings in broader astrophysical context (reionization, seed black hole problem). Meets all editorial standards. APPROVE without corrections.
